We're Gonna Need a Bigger Boat

Julien Borrel - codelake.ai
Julien Borrel, codelake.ai, 18-02-2026 - markdown

Rethinking Version Control, IDEs, and Workflows for the Agentic Development Era


Figure 1: The Current State Of Affairs. Credits: NanoBanana, Universal Pictures.


PREMISE & ASSUMPTION

The AI code generation problem will be solved within ~3/5 years. Everything that follows is derived from that single assumption.

Your personal take about this may fall into one of four categories:

  1. Yep sure ! → read on.
  2. Probably → read on.
  3. Maybe in 10 or 20 years → read this as a thought experiment. The timeline changes, the logic doesn't.
  4. Will Never Happen → this article has nothing for you.

ABSTRACT

We assume that Large Language Model code generation is, for practical purposes, a solved problem.

What remains unsolved (and largely unexamined) is every other part of the developer experience that was designed around the assumption that humans write code by hand.

Version control, code review, IDEs, debugging workflows, collaboration models, and even the concept of « source code » itself are all built on a mental model that is rapidly becoming obsolete.

This paper reasons from first principles about the cascading consequences of fully capable AI code generation on the entire software development stack, and proposes concrete architectures for the systems that must replace or radically augment what we have today.

Table of Contents

  1. The Premise: Code Generation Is Solved
  2. The First Crack: Git Is Breaking
  3. New Primitives for a New Era
  4. Weave: A Complete Version Control System for Agentic Development
  5. The Pragmatic Bridge: Building on Git
  6. Engineering Weave: Architecture and Implementation
  7. The Studio: Reimagining the IDE for Intent-Driven Development
  8. The Endgame: Code Becomes a Compiled Artifact
  9. Navigating the Transition
  10. Conclusion: Versioning What Was Meant, Not What Was Written

1. The Premise: Code Generation Is Solved

Let us begin with a stipulation that would have seemed absurd five years ago and now feels almost conservative: LLM-based code generation works. Not perfectly, not for every edge case, but well enough that the bottleneck in software development has shifted decisively away from writing code and toward everything that surrounds it.

Today, tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot Workspace can take a natural language description of a feature, a bug fix, or a refactoring goal, and produce thousands of lines of working, tested code in minutes. A single Claude Code session can generate over 3,000 diffs - more changes than many human developers produce in a week of focused work.

This is not a marginal improvement in developer productivity. It is a category change in how code comes into existence. And when the means of production change this fundamentally, the entire infrastructure of production must follow.

The tools we use today - git, VS Code, GitHub pull requests, CI/CD pipelines, even programming languages themselves - were designed for a world where a human thinks, then types, then commits, then reviews, then deploys. Every assumption embedded in these tools reflects that workflow. When the "types" step is replaced by "an AI generates 3,000 lines in 90 seconds," those assumptions don't just become suboptimal.

They become wrong.

This paper is about what comes next.

2. The First Crack: Git Is Breaking

The tension between agentic code generation and existing tooling is most immediately visible in version control. Git is where the pain is sharpest, and examining that pain reveals the deeper structural mismatch that pervades the entire toolchain.

2.1 Git's Mental Model

Git was designed around a theory of software development so foundational that it's usually invisible: code is written incrementally by humans who can narrate their intentions. The atomic unit (the commit) maps to a discrete human intention. Commit messages narrate the why behind a diff that a human peer will later review. Branches model parallel lines of human effort. Merges reconcile those efforts at the textual level.

This model has served us extraordinarily well for two decades. But it encodes assumptions that agent-generated code violates systematically.

2.2 What Breaks

The commit as a unit of meaning

A 3,000-line diff from an agent session isn't one "thought." But it's also not 200 separate thoughts. It's one goal executed through hundreds of mechanical steps. Git has no native concept for this hierarchy. You're left choosing between a single incomprehensible mega-commit and hundreds of meaningless micro-commits.

Commit messages become absurd

What do you write for a 3,000-line agent-generated diff? "Claude refactored auth"? That's useless. A detailed message for every micro-change? That's noise. The commit message format assumes a 1:1 mapping between human intent and code change that no longer exists.

Code review collapses

git diff showing 3,000 lines of agent-generated changes is essentially unreadable. The reviewer needs to understand what the agent was told to do and whether it did it correctly - not trace every line. The diff-based review model fails because it answers the wrong question. The question isn't "is this code correct line-by-line?" but rather "does this code achieve the stated goal?"

Blame and bisect lose signal

git blame showing "Claude session 2026-02-14" on 400 files tells you nothing useful. git bisect works mechanically but the commits it isolates may be meaningless intermediate states that don't even compile.

Branching semantics get weird

An agent session is neither a "feature branch" nor a series of logical commits. It's closer to a build artifact - the output of a process, not a narrative of human development. Our branching models assume human-paced, human-structured work.

2.3 The Deeper Diagnosis

These aren't surface-level inconveniences. They're symptoms of a fundamental mismatch: git versions code as a human-authored artifact, but code is increasingly a machine-derived artifact. The entire UX layer of git - commit messages, diffs, blame, log, bisect - is designed to help humans understand other humans' work. When the work is done by an agent, these tools present information in a format optimized for a question nobody is asking.

The fix isn't better commit messages or smarter diff algorithms. The fix is new primitives.

3. New Primitives for a New Era

If we reason from first principles about what version control needs to be when the dominant "author" of code is a generative AI, we arrive at a fundamentally different set of primitives.

Let us consider what changes when AI writes all the code:

Code becomes closer to a compiled artifact

The human writes intent - in natural language, specs, tests, examples. The AI produces code. The code is an intermediate representation between human intent and machine execution, much like assembly language is today. You don't version-control assembly by hand.

The source of truth shifts

Today, code is the source of truth. In this new world, intent is the source of truth and code is a derivation from intent. This is the single most profound conceptual shift in the entire analysis.

Reproducibility changes meaning

Today, the promise is: same source → same binary. Tomorrow: same intent + same model + same context → similar but possibly different code. Generation is non-deterministic. The system must handle this gracefully.

The unit of collaboration changes

Developers don't collaborate on lines of code. They collaborate on goals, constraints, specifications, and architectural decisions. The merge problem moves up a layer of abstraction.

These observations lead us to five new primitives:

3.1 Intent

The atomic source of truth.

An intent is a human-authored (or human-approved) declaration of what should be true about the system:

Intent {
    id:          content-hash
    author:      human identity
    type:        goal | constraint | spec | test | architectural_decision
    content:     natural language + optional formal spec
    priority:    required | preferred | aspirational
    scope:       global | module("auth") | file("src/api/routes.ts")
    parent:      optional Intent.id
    supersedes:  optional Intent.id
    created_at:  timestamp
}

Intents come in several flavors:

  • A goal declares a desired capability ("The system should authenticate users via OAuth2 with Google and GitHub providers").

  • A constraint declares a non-functional requirement ("All API responses must complete within 200ms at p99").

  • A spec details behavior ("The /users endpoint accepts GET with pagination and POST with email validation").

  • A test declares an observable property ("When a token expires mid-request, the system should refresh transparently without returning a 401").

  • An architectural decision records a structural choice ("We use event sourcing for the payments domain, CRUD for everything else").

Crucially, intents form a DAG, not a linear history. A constraint can apply to multiple goals. A spec can refine an architectural decision.

This is how humans actually think about systems: not as sequences of changes, but as overlapping, sometimes-conflicting declarations about desired behavior.

3.2 Derivation

A derivation is a recorded transformation from intents to code. This is what replaces the commit:

Derivation {
    id:            content-hash
    inputs:        [Intent.id]
    agent:         AgentIdentity {model, version, config}
    context:       ContextSnapshot
    output:        Snapshot.id
    transcript:    content-hash
    verification:  Verification.id
    confidence:    float
    deterministic: bool
    cost:          {tokens, time, money}
    created_at:    timestamp
}

The difference from a commit: a derivation records the full causal chain.

Not "what changed" but "why it changed, what produced the change, what verified the change, and how confident we are in it."

A derivation is reproducible-in-principle -> you can re-run it and get a functionally equivalent (though possibly textually different) output.

No commit message needed.

  • The intent is the message
  • The derivation is the audit trail.

3.3 Snapshot

The actual code state at a point in time. This is closest to git's tree object, but enriched with structural metadata:

Snapshot {
    id:           content-hash
    files:        MerkleTree
    manifest: {
        intent_coverage:  Map<Intent.id, CoverageStatus>
        module_map:       Map<ModuleName, [File]>
        dependency_graph: Graph<Module, Module>
        api_surface:      StructuredAPIDescription
        type_signature:   GlobalTypeMap
    }
    derived_from: Derivation.id
}

The manifest is key.

Every snapshot knows which intents it satisfies, how modules relate, and what its API surface looks like. This enables semantic operations (merging, diffing, reviewing) at the intent level rather than the text level.

3.4 Verification

A first-class record of proof that a snapshot satisfies its intents:

Verification {
    id:       content-hash
    snapshot: Snapshot.id
    checks: [
        {
            intent:   Intent.id,
            method:   test | type_check | formal_proof | benchmark | human_review,
            result:   pass | fail | partial | inconclusive,
            evidence: content-hash,
            coverage: float
        }
    ]
    overall_status: verified | partial | unverified
    verified_by:    agent | human | ci_pipeline
    created_at:     timestamp
}

In git, tests are external - you run them, but the result isn't part of the version history. In this system, verification is a first-class primitive. You can query: "Show me the last snapshot where all intents were fully verified." You can enforce policies: "No snapshot reaches production without human-reviewed verification of all required intents."

This also redefines "regression." A regression isn't "a test broke", it's "a previously satisfied intent is no longer covered." That's a much richer and more actionable signal.

3.5 Perspective

This replaces branches, but the concept is fundamentally different.

A perspective is a view of the system filtered and prioritized by a set of intents:

Perspective {
    id:               content-hash
    name:             string
    intents:          [Intent.id]
    current_snapshot: Snapshot.id
    policy: {
        auto_derive:           bool
        verification_required: Verification.level
        human_approval:        required | optional | none
    }
    subscribers:      [Identity]
}

Why not branches ?

Because branches assume the unit of divergence is code. In an intent-driven world, divergence happens at the intent level. Two teams might share 90% of their intents but disagree on one architectural decision. A perspective lets them each see a codebase derived from their own intent set, without forking the entire history.

"Production" is a perspective. "Staging" is a perspective. "What if we used GraphQL instead of REST" is a perspective. Perspectives can be cheaply materialized - since code is derivable from intents, you don't need to maintain separate code histories.

You just re-derive when the perspective's intent set changes.

4. Weave: A Complete Version Control System for Agentic Development

With these primitives defined, we can specify the operations of a complete system.

We call it Weave, where human intent and machine craft interweave.

4.1 Core Operations

Instead of git commit

weave derive An agent reads current intents plus the current snapshot, produces a new snapshot satisfying updated intents, records the full derivation with transcript, and runs verification automatically.

Instead of git diff

weave delta Rather than showing line-level diffs, weave delta presents semantic changes by default:

Intent changes:
  + Added: "Support WebSocket connections for real-time updates"
  ~ Modified: "API responses within 200ms" → "API responses within 100ms"

Derived impact:
  3 modules affected (api, websocket [new], events)
  17 files regenerated
  API surface: +4 endpoints, 1 modified

Verification delta:
  Previously: 24/24 intents verified
  Now: 23/25 intents verified (2 new intents pending verification)

Line-level diffs exist but they're a drill-down, not the default. The interesting delta is between intents and between verification states, not between text.

Instead of git merge:

weave reconcile Reconciliation works at the intent level.
It identifies overlapping vs. unique intents, detects contradictions (not textual conflicts but semantic conflicts), and for non-conflicting intent sets, simply re-derives from the union. For actual conflicts, it presents the intent-level tension to a human:

Conflict detected:
  Perspective A intent: "Use REST for all public APIs"
  Perspective B intent: "Use GraphQL for the dashboard API"

  These intents conflict for scope: module("dashboard-api")

  Options:
  [1] Keep A (REST everywhere)
  [2] Keep B (GraphQL for dashboard)
  [3] Reconcile: "REST for all APIs except dashboard, which uses GraphQL"
  [4] Defer: keep both perspectives separate

No three-way text merge. No conflict markers. No manual resolution of import statement collisions. The human resolves intent conflicts and the agent re-derives the code.

Instead of git blame

weave trace It doesn't show "who wrote line 47."
It shows the full causal chain from behavior to intent to derivation:

$ weave trace --behavior "what happens when a token expires"

Behavior: Token expiry handling

  Intent: "When a token expires mid-request, refresh transparently
           without returning 401" (created by: alice, 2026-02-10)

  Derived by: claude-opus-4-6 in Derivation d8f2a...
  Confidence: 0.94

  Implemented across:
    src/auth/oauth.ts       → refreshToken(), isTokenExpired()
    src/middleware/auth.ts   → tokenRefreshMiddleware()
    src/auth/oauth.test.ts  → 4 test cases

  Verification: ✓ All 4 test cases passing
                ✓ Load test confirms no 401s during refresh

  Dependencies:
    ← Relies on Intent "OAuth2 with Google and GitHub"
    → Required by Intent "All API calls authenticated"

This answers the question developers actually ask: "Why does this work this way and can I trust it?"

4.2 Novel Operations

Beyond replacing git's existing operations, Weave introduces operations that have no git equivalent because they were never possible before.

weave challenge takes a hypothetical and evaluates the current system against it:

$ weave challenge "What if we need to support 10x current traffic?"

Intents at risk:
  ⚠ "API responses within 200ms at p99" - current architecture
     uses synchronous DB calls in 3 hot paths. At 10x load,
     p99 likely exceeds 800ms.

  ⚠ "Token refresh transparent to client" - refresh endpoint
     is single-threaded. At 10x concurrent refreshes,
     likely bottleneck.

  ✓ "Event sourcing for payments" - append-only, scales linearly.

Suggested new intents:
  + "Hot-path DB calls must be async with connection pooling"
  + "Token refresh must support concurrent requests with dedup"

Estimated derivation cost to address: ~40k tokens, ~15 files

Challenge turns the version control system into a reasoning partner about the codebase. It's possible because intents are machine-readable and the system understands the full derivation chain from intent to code.

weave reintent is the inverse of derivation. You modify an intent and the system cascades the change:

$ weave reintent "Simplify - we no longer need GitHub OAuth, only Google"

Intent change: Remove "GitHub OAuth provider"

Cascade analysis:
  3 intents reference GitHub auth
  2 derivations produced GitHub-specific code
  Impact: ~12 files affected, 1 module can be removed entirely

  Verification: 8 test cases will be removed,
                2 test cases need updating

  Estimated re-derivation: ~15k tokens, ~30 seconds

  [Proceed] [Preview derived changes] [Cancel]

Code is re-derived from updated intents, not manually edited. The system handles the cascade. This is refactoring elevated to its logical conclusion: changing what the system should do and letting the machine figure out the implementation.

4.3 The Storage Layer

Underneath, Weave's storage model borrows what's good from git while discarding what no longer serves:

From git, keep:

  • content-addressable storage (everything hashed),
  • Merkle trees for file snapshots (efficient deduplication),
  • DAG structure (intents and derivations both form DAGs),
  • distributed/local-first operation.

From git, discard:

  • linear commit history as the primary narrative,
  • text-diff as the primary comparison mechanism,
  • branches as the divergence model,
  • commit messages as the documentation mechanism.

New storage structures:

  • Intent Index: a searchable, queryable index of all intents with their relationships, scopes, and statuses,
  • Derivation Cache: since re-derivation is expensive (LLM calls), results are cached keyed by (intent-set-hash, agent-version),
  • Verification Store: first-class storage of test results, proofs, and review decisions linked to snapshots,
  • Semantic Index: embeddings of intents and code for natural-language queries ("find everything related to authentication").

5. The Pragmatic Bridge: Building on Git

A complete replacement for git is a massive undertaking.

The critical architectural question is: can Weave be built on top of git, using it as an invisible storage engine?

5.1 What Git Actually Is

Strip away the UX and git is three things:

  • a content-addressable object store (blobs, trees, commits, all hashed by SHA),
  • a DAG of snapshots (each commit points to a full tree state plus parents),
  • and a ref system (branches, tags, HEAD are just pointers into the DAG).

Git doesn't inherently care about "commit messages as human narratives" or "diffs as the review unit."

Those are conventions and tooling layered on top. The underlying engine is actually a very capable versioned snapshot store. This is encouraging - much of what feels broken is the porcelain, not the plumbing.

5.2 What Maps Cleanly

Sessions → squashed commits + metadata

Each agent session becomes one squashed commit with structured trailers:

weave-goal: Migrate auth module to OAuth2
weave-session: 2026-02-14T09:32:00Z
weave-model: claude-opus-4-6
weave-tests: 94/100 passing
weave-files-changed: 47

Goals → branches or tag groups

A "goal" maps to a branch that gets squash-merged into main, preserving session-level detail on the branch while presenting a clean goal-level history on trunk.

Provenance → git notes

git notes lets you attach arbitrary data to any commit without changing its hash - perfect for transcripts, token usage, test results, and prompts.

Semantic diffs → custom diff drivers

Git supports custom diff tools. A weave diff command can call an agent to produce a semantic summary, presenting it first with line-level diffs available on drill-down.

5.3 Where Git Strains

Transcript storage

Agent transcripts can be tens of thousands of tokens. Git notes work but bloat the repo. A sidecar database (SQLite, indexed by commit SHA) is more practical for heavy metadata.

Goal-level rollback with dependencies

"Revert this goal" is easy if squash-merged, but if goals have dependencies, git has no way to express or enforce that. The dependency graph is Weave's responsibility, not git's.

Semantic merge conflict resolution

A custom merge driver could call an agent, but network calls during merge introduce non-determinism and cost. It works architecturally but feels fragile.

Session replay

"Replay this session against a different branch" means re-running an agent with the same prompt against different code. Git stores outcomes, not processes. This is fundamentally outside git's model.

Real-time collaboration on goals

If two developers' agents are working on related goals simultaneously, ideal coordination requires live awareness. Git is batch-oriented and offline-first. This kind of live coordination requires a layer above git entirely.

5.4 The Resulting Architecture

Git doesn't need to be replaced - it needs to be demoted. Today, git is both the storage engine and the user-facing abstraction.

Weave keeps it as the engine but takes over the abstraction layer:

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│          Weave CLI / UI             │  ← what humans interact with
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Weave Core Logic           │  ← goals, sessions, dependencies,
│  • goal tracking                    │    semantic diffs, replay, review
│  • session orchestration            │
│  • semantic diff generation         │
│  • dependency graph                 │
│  • merge reconciliation             │
├──────────────┬──────────────────────┤
│   Git        │   Sidecar Store      │
│  (plumbing)  │   (SQLite/service)   │
│  • snapshots │  • transcripts       │
│  • DAG       │  • full prompts      │
│  • refs      │  • test histories    │
│  • notes     │  • dependency graph  │
│  • trailers  │  • agent metadata    │
└──────────────┴──────────────────────┘

This is analogous to how databases work: nobody interacts with B-trees directly. SQL is the abstraction; the storage engine is invisible. Weave is the SQL to git's B-tree.

6. Engineering Weave: Architecture and Implementation

6.1 Choosing the Right Language

Weave's nature is unusual. It's not a database, not a web app, not a CLI tool - it's a developer workflow orchestrator sitting between humans, agents, and git.

That shapes the language choice profoundly.

What Weave actually does at runtime:

  • orchestrates agent sessions (spawns processes, streams output, manages context)
  • talks to git plumbing (commits, branches, notes, diffs)
  • talks to LLM APIs (for semantic diffs, merge reconciliation, summary generation)
  • manages a sidecar store (transcripts, dependency graphs, goal metadata)
  • presents a CLI and eventually a TUI/GUI
  • and integrates with existing ecosystems (editors, CI/CD, GitHub/GitLab APIs).

The bottleneck is never Weave's own computation. It's always waiting on git operations, network calls to LLMs, or file I/O. This is an I/O-bound coordination layer, not a compute-bound system.

Rust: would give speed that isn't needed and fight on everything Weave needs to do well - rapid prototyping, flexible data structures, easy HTTP work, quick iteration. The compile times alone would kill iteration speed.

Python: is great for prototyping with excellent LLM ecosystem support, but distribution is painful (Python environment management as a prerequisite for a dev tool is a non-starter), the concurrency model is awkward for Weave's needs, and startup time matters for a CLI invoked dozens of times a day.

TypeScript: (on Node.js or Bun) hits a surprising sweet spot.

  • Distribution is solved (npm install -g weave),
  • the entire runtime is built for I/O-bound coordination,
  • the ecosystem is right (simple-git for git operations, every LLM provider has a first-class TS SDK, ink for TUI rendering),
  • types catch workflow bugs without Rust's ceremony.

And there's a practical precedent: Claude Code itself ships as TypeScript.

6.2 System Structure 

weave/
├── packages/
│   ├── core/                  # The brain
│   │   ├── intents/           # Intent lifecycle, DAG management
│   │   ├── sessions/          # Session orchestration, state machine
│   │   ├── git/               # Git plumbing abstraction layer
│   │   ├── store/             # Sidecar DB (SQLite via better-sqlite3)
│   │   ├── agents/            # LLM provider adapters
│   │   └── semantic/          # Semantic diff, merge, summarization
│   │
│   ├── cli/                   # CLI entry point
│   │   ├── commands/          # weave goal, weave session, weave review...
│   │   └── tui/               # Interactive terminal UI (ink)
│   │
│   ├── integrations/          # Platform adapters
│   │   ├── github/
│   │   ├── gitlab/
│   │   └── vscode/
│   │
│   └── sdk/                   # For plugins/extensions
│       └── types/             # Shared type definitions
│
├── weave.config.ts            # Per-repo configuration
└── .weave/                    # Per-repo sidecar (alongside .git)
    ├── weave.db               # SQLite: intents, sessions, dependencies
    ├── transcripts/           # Agent transcripts (content-addressed)
    └── cache/                 # Semantic diff cache

6.3 The Sidecar Database

The .weave directory lives alongside .git and stores everything git can't handle efficiently:

CREATE TABLE intents (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    type TEXT NOT NULL,          -- goal | constraint | spec | test | arch_decision
    content TEXT NOT NULL,
    priority TEXT DEFAULT 'required',
    scope TEXT,
    parent_intent_id TEXT,
    supersedes_intent_id TEXT,
    status TEXT DEFAULT 'active',
    created_at DATETIME,
    updated_at DATETIME
);

CREATE TABLE derivations (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    intent_ids TEXT NOT NULL,    -- JSON array of intent IDs
    git_commit_sha TEXT,
    model TEXT,
    prompt TEXT,
    summary TEXT,
    transcript_hash TEXT,       -- pointer into transcripts/
    confidence REAL,
    tokens_used INTEGER,
    files_changed INTEGER,
    cost_usd REAL,
    started_at DATETIME,
    ended_at DATETIME
);

CREATE TABLE verifications (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    snapshot_sha TEXT,
    derivation_id TEXT REFERENCES derivations(id),
    checks TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON array of check results
    overall_status TEXT,
    verified_by TEXT,
    created_at DATETIME
);

CREATE TABLE perspectives (
    id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    intent_ids TEXT NOT NULL,
    current_snapshot_sha TEXT,
    policy TEXT,                -- JSON policy object
    git_branch TEXT,
    created_at DATETIME
);

CREATE TABLE intent_dependencies (
    intent_id TEXT REFERENCES intents(id),
    depends_on_intent_id TEXT REFERENCES intents(id),
    relationship TEXT           -- builds-on | conflicts | related
);

6.4 The CLI Surface

Minimal, opinionated, progressive disclosure:

# Intents
weave intent create "Migrate auth to OAuth2"      # create an intent
weave intent list                                   # see active intents
weave intent refine <id> "Add rate limiting"        # refine an existing intent
weave intent retire <id>                            # mark no longer needed

# Goals (high-level grouping of intents)
weave goal create "Q3 Auth Overhaul"               # group related intents
weave goal complete                                 # verify and close

# Sessions (agent invocations within a goal)
weave session start "rewrite the token logic"       # begin agent session
weave session list                                  # sessions in current goal
weave session replay <session-id>                   # re-run against current state

# Review
weave review                        # semantic diff of current goal vs main
weave review <goal-id>              # review a specific goal
weave review --detail               # include line-level diffs
weave review --transcript           # show agent reasoning

# Understanding
weave trace <file>                  # why does this file exist?
weave trace --behavior "token exp"  # trace a behavior through the system
weave why <file>                    # shorthand for trace
weave history                       # intent-level history (not commit log)
weave deps                          # visualize intent dependency graph

# Exploration
weave challenge "GDPR compliance"   # evaluate system against a hypothetical
weave reintent "Drop GitHub OAuth"  # modify intents and cascade

# Verification
weave verify                        # run all verification checks
weave fitness                       # intent coverage dashboard

# Escape hatch
weave git <any git command>         # pass-through to underlying git

6.5 The Agent Interface

Weave wraps rather than replaces code generation agents.

The interface between Weave and agents is an event protocol:

interface AgentEvents {
    onSessionStart(intent: Intent, context: Snapshot): void;
    onFileChange(path: string, type: 'create' | 'modify' | 'delete'): void;
    onTestRun(results: TestResults): void;
    onAgentReasoning(transcript: string): void;
    onSessionEnd(summary: string, confidence: number): void;
}

Any agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Aider, Copilot Workspace) can implement this protocol. For agents that don't natively support it, Weave falls back to file-watching and process monitoring.

Figure 2: Are we there yet?.

7. Weave Studio: Reimagining the IDE for Intent-Driven Development

If version control must change, so must the environment where developers spend their days.

Today's IDEs are text editors with superpowers:

  • The file tree is the spine,
  • the tab bar shows open files,
  • the editor pane shows lines of code
  • everything (navigation, search, debugging, refactoring) is organized around: where in the text am I?

In the intent-driven world, this is like navigating a city by its plumbing schematics. The plumbing is real and important, but nobody thinks about a city that way.

You think in neighborhoods, landmarks, routes, purposes.

The new IDE - Weave Studio - is organized around: what am I trying to make true about this system?

7.1 Three Realms

Weave Studio replaces the file tree + editor + terminal triptych with three realms that the developer moves fluidly between. These aren't fixed panels, they're modes of attention that the Studio reconfigures around.

The Intent Realm

"What Should Be True"

This is the home screen. Not a file tree - an intent graph displayed as a spatial canvas. Related intents cluster together. Connections show dependencies and refinements. Visual encoding communicates status at a glance: bright solid nodes for fully verified intents; pulsing nodes for intents currently being derived; amber outlines for partial verification; dim nodes for aspirational intents not yet derived; red nodes for broken intents that lost verification.

The developer's primary navigation is through this space. Click an intent and the entire UI re-orients: the system realm zooms to relevant modules, the derivation realm shows implementing code and transcripts.

Intent authoring - the central creative act - gets a rich editor closer to a specification tool than a text editor, with structured fields for type, scope, priority, acceptance criteria, and refinement relationships. Before deriving, an "Explore Impact" action shows which modules, files, and existing intents would be affected.

Intents also support a conversation mode where the developer discusses refinements with the agent. These conversations become part of the intent record - versioned context explaining why the intent has the shape it does.

Future developers (or agents) reading the intent understand not just what but the trade-offs considered.

The System Realm

"What Is True"

This is where you understand the current state of the system.

→ Not files but behaviors, modules, flows, and relationships.

The default view is a behavioral topology:

A visual representation of what the system does, organized by domain. Each module shows its behaviors, not its files. Click a behavior and you see its intents, verification status, API surface, dependencies, and (if you want) the derived code.

A flow view:

Visualizes runtime behavior. Select "user logs in with expired token" and see the complete request path through the system, with each step linked to its implementing intent, code, and tests. These are living diagrams generated from the system's understanding of its own structure, not manually maintained documentation.

A persistent fitness dashboard:

Provides ambient system health - not CI status, but intent fitness: how many intents are verified, what's broken, what's stale, what's the overall confidence level. It's always visible, like a vital signs monitor.

The Derivation Realm

"How It Became True"

Code lives here, but it's no longer the protagonist. It's evidence that supports intents.

When viewing code, the frame is different from a traditional editor.

Every file carries a header showing its provenance: which intent it derives from, which agent produced it, confidence level, verification status, last derivation time. The code itself is slightly de-emphasized as the output of a process, not the creative work product.

Instead of comments, derived code carries intent links → live connections to the intent graph that explain why each section of code exists. Click one and navigate to the full intent context.

The transcript viewer shows the agent's reasoning for any derivation: options considered, trade-offs evaluated, risks identified. This captured rationale (normally lost when a developer closes their browser) becomes permanent, versioned documentation.

7.2 The Review Experience

The default review view isn't a line-level diff.

It's a behavioral delta:

BEHAVIORAL CHANGES

  New behaviors:
    + Auth endpoints reject after 10 failed attempts per IP per minute
    + 429 responses include Retry-After header
    + Rate limit counters persist in PostgreSQL

  Modified behaviors:
    ~ Auth middleware checks rate limit before token validation

RISKS FLAGGED
    ⚠ Potential latency tension at high load

VERIFICATION
    ✓ 4 new test cases passing
    ✓ All 24 existing tests still pass

    ▸ Show file-level changes (8 files)
    ▸ Show line-level diff (+147, -12)

Line-level diffs are two clicks deep, not the default. The reviewer's question is "did the agent achieve the intent correctly and safely?" - not "is line 47 syntactically correct?" Most reviews never need line-level detail.

Review also becomes generative.

When a reviewer identifies a future need or edge case, that observation becomes a new intent → tracked, derivable, part of the system's source of truth.

Review comments stop being ephemeral PR annotations and start feeding the intent graph.

7.3 Navigation Reimagined

In VS Code, navigation is: file tree → file → line → symbol. Everything is spatial and textual. In the Studio, navigation is associative and multi-modal:

By intent (primary)

"Show me everything about authentication" → intent cluster → derived modules → code → tests. Start from meaning, descend to implementation.

By behavior

"What happens when a payment fails?" → flow view → relevant intents → derived code. Start from a scenario, trace through the system.

By concern

"What's broken?" → fitness dashboard → broken intents → failing verifications → the causative derivation → the triggering intent change.

Start from a problem, find its root cause in intent space.

By natural language (the killer feature): a persistent command palette that understands the system semantically:

> "Where do we handle token expiry?"
  → Auth module → refreshToken behavior → Intent "transparent refresh"

> "What would break if I removed the sessions table?"
  → 3 intents affected, 2 modules impacted, cascade analysis

> "Why is the payments module so complex?"
  → 8 intents converge on this module, dependency analysis

> "What hasn't been verified recently?"
  → 4 intents with stale verification (>7 days), recommend re-verify

7.4 Collaboration

Presence by intent, not file

Instead of "Alice is editing auth.ts," you see "Alice is working on: Rate limiting on auth endpoints - currently deriving, agent running." When two people's intents might interact, the Studio proactively warns and suggests coordination and intent reconciliation.

The code escape hatch

The Studio includes a full code editor (sometimes you need to read or hand-edit code).

But when you edit manually, the Studio notices and gently guides you back: update the intent to match, keep as manual override (flagged for re-derivation review), or discard and re-derive.

Manual edits create tracked tension, not silent divergence.

8. The Endgame: Code Becomes a Compiled Artifact

Following these ideas to their logical conclusion leads to a prediction that sounds radical but may be inevitable within 3-5 years: generated code becomes non-editable, like compiled code.

If you want a change, you alter the specs, the rules, or introduce a challenge.

→ You don't touch the derived output.

8.1 The Convergence

Several trends converging simultaneously:

Model capabilities

Reach a point where re-deriving an entire module from intent is cheaper and more reliable than patching it by hand. We're arguably already there for small modules.

Verification matures

Formal methods, property-based testing, and AI-assisted proof become standard. When you can prove a derivation satisfies its intent, you don't need to read the code to trust it.

The economics flip

Understanding existing code is a major cost center - onboarding, debugging, refactoring. If code is re-derivable from intent, that entire cost category vanishes.

The liability argument emerges

If an agent derives code from verified intents and the code is proven correct, then a human manually editing that code introduces unverified risk.

Manual code editing becomes the equivalent of manually patching a binary in production.

8.2 The New Abstraction Stack

Today:

Human intent (informal, lossy)
  → Code (the editable artifact, source of truth)
    → Compiled binary (non-editable)
      → Machine execution

Tomorrow:

Human intent (formal, versioned, the source of truth)
  → Derived code (non-editable intermediate representation)
    → Compiled binary (non-editable)
      → Machine execution

Code doesn't disappear. It shifts one level down in the abstraction hierarchy, joining compiled binaries as something that's inspectable but not manually authored.

8.3 Programming Languages Stop Evolving ?

This is perhaps the most counterintuitive implication.

If no human reads or writes code as a primary activity, then the qualities we value in languages (readability, expressiveness, ergonomics, elegant syntax) stop mattering.

What matters instead

Can the agent generate correct, efficient, verifiable code in this language?

The language becomes an optimization target, not a creative medium.

Consequences include:

  • Agents converging on a small number of languages optimized for machine generation and formal verification, regardless of human ergonomics
  • new languages designed explicitly as agent targets, analogous to LLVM IR at a higher abstraction level,
  • existing "human" languages persisting as legacy, maintained for their cultural and historical value.

The entire field of programming language design (decades of work on syntax, type systems, paradigms) gets subsumed into compiler optimization for intent-to-execution pipelines. Rust's borrow checker, Haskell's type system, Python's readability: these were solutions to human problems.

When humans aren't writing code, those problems dissolve.

8.4 The Developer Role Bifurcates

Two distinct roles emerge from what we currently call "software developer":

System Architects

They work entirely in the intent layer. They think about what systems should do, how components interact, what trade-offs to make, what constraints to impose. They need deep domain knowledge, systems thinking, and the ability to specify unambiguous requirements. They never look at code except as a debugging curiosity. This role is closer to a product engineer or systems engineer than a traditional programmer.

Infrastructure/Verification Engineers

Build and maintain the derivation pipeline itself (the agents), the verification systems, the formal methods tooling, the execution environment. They do work with code, but it's the code of the development infrastructure, not of the applications being built. This is a much smaller number of people working at a much deeper level.

The vast middle (the millions of developers who today write application code, debug it, review it, refactor it) gets absorbed by agents.

Notably, human coding evolves into a hobby like writing poetry or playing chess, not a profession.

This is uncomfortable to state plainly, but intellectual honesty demands it.

8.5 Education Transforms

If you don't write code, what do you study?

Specification and intent formulation

How to translate fuzzy human needs into precise, verifiable intents. This is closer to requirements engineering and formal methods than to coding bootcamps.

Systems thinking

Understanding complex system behavior, emergent properties, failure modes, trade-offs. Already what senior engineers do, it just becomes the primary skill rather than a senior-level specialty.

Verification and trust

How do you know the system does what you intended? What does proof mean? What's the gap between tested and verified? Formal methods move from academic niche to core competency.

Domain knowledge

If intent is the source of truth, then understanding the domain (finance, healthcare, logistics) becomes more important than understanding implementation patterns. The best system architect for a healthcare app isn't the best coder; it's the person who understands healthcare workflows most deeply.

Algorithms and data structures become like physics for civil engineers i.e. foundational knowledge that informs intuition but isn't the daily practice.

8.6 Open Source Becomes Intent-Source

Today, open source means sharing code.

In the intent-driven world, the valuable artifact is the intent set.

An open-source "intent library" for authentication wouldn't be code you import: it would be a set of intents you adopt into your system, which your agent derives into code tailored to your specific architecture.

  • Forking means "I took your intents and modified these three for my domain."

  • Contributing means "I identified a missing intent and propose adding it."

  • Bug reports become "Your intent set doesn't account for this scenario."

The code itself is ephemeral.

You don't vendor it, pin versions of it, or read its source. You trust the intent set + derivation pipeline + verification layer.

If a better agent comes along, you re-derive the entire codebase from the same intents and get better code for free.

8.7 Technical Debt Dissolves

Technical debt is accumulated human decisions that were expedient at the time but costly to maintain. When code is re-derived from intents, code-level debt disappears. Bad variable names, tangled dependencies, copy-pasted logic - all artifacts of human coding that don't survive re-derivation. Every derivation produces fresh code from current intents.

Intent-level debt replaces it

Conflicting intents, underspecified requirements, missing constraints → these are the new form of debt. But they're visible and explicit in the intent graph, unlike code debt which hides in implementation details.

Architectural debt becomes a conversation

"We chose REST but should migrate to gRPC" is an intent-level change. Modify the architectural intent, re-derive. The migration that would take a team six months takes an agent an afternoon.

8.8 Debugging Inverts

Today

Something's broken → read stack trace → find line of code → understand logic → identify bug → fix code → test.

Tomorrow

Something's broken → check which intent's verification failed → examine the intent for underspecification → refine the intent → re-derive → verify.

You never ask "what's wrong with line 47?"

You ask "what's wrong with the intent, or what's missing from the intent, such that the derived behavior is incorrect?"

And if the intent is correct but the derivation is wrong, that's a bug in the agent, not in your system. You report it upstream (or switch agents) and re-derive.

8.9 Regulatory Compliance Improves

If code is non-editable and derived from intents, then the intent set becomes the auditable artifact. For regulated industries, this is better than the status quo. Auditors review intents (human-readable, domain-specific) instead of code. Every derivation has a full transcript explaining the agent's reasoning. Verification is first-class. The chain from regulatory requirement → intent → derived code → verification proof is fully traceable.

SOX compliance, FDA software validation, financial regulations → all become more tractable when the source of truth is a set of verifiable intents rather than millions of lines of inscrutable code.

9. Navigating the Transition

9.1 The Hybrid Period

The vision described here doesn't arrive overnight. For the next 3-5 years, we inhabit a hybrid world where some code is human-written and some is agent-derived; intent formulation is informal and inconsistent; verification is incomplete; developers are uncomfortable not editing code; and organizations aren't sure what roles to hire for.

This is why building Weave as a layer on top of git matters now.

It provides the bridge → formalizing intents and derivations incrementally, on existing repos, without requiring anyone to go cold turkey on code editing.

Teams can discover the intent-driven workflow gradually, building confidence before committing fully.

9.2 An Incremental Adoption Path

Week 1 → Install Weave

weave init in an existing git repo. Start using weave goal create and weave session start to wrap agent coding sessions. Everything falls back to standard git for anything Weave doesn't handle.

Month 1 → Structured metadata

The team gets used to structured commit messages with intent trailers. weave review starts generating semantic diffs. weave history replaces git log for understanding what happened and why.

Month 3 → Intent-first workflow

Developers begin writing intents before invoking agents. weave trace becomes the go-to for understanding code provenance. Code review shifts from line-level to behavioral-level.

Month 6 → Verification culture

Verification becomes first-class. The fitness dashboard is always visible. Policies emerge: "no unverified intents reach production." The team starts thinking about the system in terms of intents rather than files.

Year 1 → Code as derived artifact

The mental model shift completes. Developers spend most of their time in the intent realm. Manual code editing becomes the exception, not the rule. Re-derivation is routine. Weave Studio replaces VS Code for daily work.

9.3 What to Build First

A MVP, achievable in weeks, would include:

  • weave goal create/list/complete for goal lifecycle backed by SQLite and git branches
  • weave session start/end wrapping a shell session, capturing file changes, auto-committing,
  • weave trail for structured commit messages,
  • weave review calling an LLM API to generate semantic diff summaries,
  • weave history for goal-level log instead of git log.

That's enough to be useful today, on real repos, with zero migration cost.

Conclusion

Today's worldview → code is the artifact, history is a sequence of changes to that artifact, humans are the authors.

Agentic coding worldview → intent is the artifact, code is a derivation, history is the evolution of intent, verification is the proof of correctness, and authorship is collaboration between human directors and machine agents.

The deepest change isn't technical.

It's that we stop versioning what was written and start versioning what was meant. Code becomes ephemeral. Intent becomes permanent. The tools that track, verify, and evolve intent are the tools that matter.

The 3,000-line diff from a Claude Code session isn't a problem to solve with a better commit message. It's a signal that the abstraction layer is wrong. The commit message is unanswerable because the question it's trying to answer - "what did a human intend when they typed these lines?" - no longer applies.

The right question is: "What should be true about this system, is it true, and can we prove it?" Answering that question demands new primitives (intents, derivations, verifications, perspectives), new operations (derive, reconcile, trace, challenge, reintent), new tools (Weave for version control, Weave Studio for the development environment), and ultimately, a new understanding of what software development is.

The people and organizations who internalize this transition early - who start thinking in intents, who build verification practices, who learn to direct rather than implement - will have an enormous advantage when the threshold arrives. And the threshold is closer than most people think.

We built our current tools for a world where humans write code into files at low frequency and low volume. That world is ending.

We're gonna need a bigger boat.

This paper emerged from a brainstorming session with a team of seasoned developers, exploring the implications of the "solved code generation" hypothesis. It represents a snapshot of thinking at a moment when the transition from human-authored to machine-derived code is accelerating but not yet complete - a moment when the right architectural decisions could shape the next era of software development.


Figure 3: Do we need a bigger machete as well?
